nativeRange.habitat: The Rubber Pleco is native to fast-flowing, highly oxygenated foothill streams and rivers in the Andes, where rocky substrate, strong current, and clear, cool water are characteristic. Its natural distribution spans highland drainages of both the Orinoco and Magdalena river systems.

decorPreferences: smooth river rocks and slate for grazing surfaces
caves and rock overhangs as shelters
strong directional powerhead or circulation pump to simulate current
minimal fine substrate (coarse sand or fine gravel acceptable)
live or artificial plants to supplement biofilm surfaces
avoid sharp-edged decor that can abrade the underside

diet.notes: The Rubber Pleco is a dedicated aufwuchs-grazer — a term for the community of algae, biofilm, and microscopic organisms that coat submerged hard surfaces — and feeds almost exclusively by rasping these from rocks, driftwood, and glass. In the aquarium, this diet should be supported with sinking algae wafers, spirulina wafers, and blanched vegetables such as courgette, cucumber, spinach, and peas. Soft-cooked sweet potato and squash are also accepted. A small amount of protein-based food such as bloodworm can be offered occasionally, but should not dominate the diet. This species grazes throughout the day, so tanks should ideally have established algae growth on rocks and glass; when that is limited, prepared foods should be offered as a regular supplement.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras tolerant of cooler water (ember tetras, cardinal tetras at the upper range)
hillstream loaches (Sewellia, Gastromyzon) sharing similar flow and temperature requirements
cooler-water corydoras species (C. sterbai is too warm; prefer C. panda, C. trilineatus)
mountain cloud minnows (White Cloud Mountain Minnow)
other non-competing plecos of different size
avoidWith: tropical fish requiring temperatures above 26°C (discus, altum angels, most South American cichlids)
large aggressive cichlids that may attack the pleco
other Chaetostoma spp. or similarly shaped plecos competing for the same territory and grazing space in smaller tanks
fish requiring soft, still water (incompatible oxygenation needs)
fast-moving nippy species that harass the pleco’s fleshy fins

imageCandidates.notes: Ictiologia Universidad Catolica de Oriente; 4000x2087. In-tank underwater side view, sharp; clearly shows the flattened bulldog head, matte spotted rubber-like skin, stocky body, and absence of nasal tentacles.
Frank Alvarez; 640x480. Specimen held beside its Andean stream habitat; strong lateral view of the broad bulldog head profile and olive-tan barred body. Lower resolution than the recommended pick.
Frank Alvarez; 640x289. Top-down view on a tray showing the full spotted dorsal pattern and broad head; out-of-water and low resolution, so kept only as a supplementary angle.

summary: The Rubber Pleco is a compact, matte-skinned hillstream catfish from Andean foothill streams in Colombia and Venezuela. Unlike most plecos sold in the hobby, it requires cool temperatures, high dissolved oxygen, and strong water movement, making it an intermediate-level species best suited to cool-water community aquariums.
careNotes:
The Rubber Pleco (Chaetostoma milesi) originates from fast-flowing Andean foothill streams in Colombia and Venezuela, making it one of the more demanding pleco species with respect to water conditions. Unlike the widely sold Common Pleco or Bristlenose Pleco, it requires cool temperatures (20–24°C), high dissolved oxygen, and strong water movement — conditions closer to a hillstream loach setup than a typical tropical community tank. Temperatures above 26°C for extended periods are associated with lethargy, disease susceptibility, and premature death; this is the most common husbandry error reported with this species. A strong powerhead or circulation pump positioned to create meaningful surface agitation and directional flow is essential. High oxygen levels must be maintained, particularly during warm weather; a chiller may be necessary in summer for indoor setups in warm climates.

The Rubber Pleco is distinguished from the Bristlenose Pleco (Ancistrus spp.) by the absence of nasal tentacles (bristles), its distinctively broad and flattened "bulldog" head profile, and its thick matte, rubber-like skin texture. It is also stockier and less elongated than most other members of the family Loricariidae encountered in the hobby.

As a dedicated aufwuchs-grazer, this species feeds continuously on algae and biofilm throughout the day. Tanks should ideally have well-established algae growth on rocks, glass, and driftwood before introduction. Supplement regularly with sinking algae wafers, spirulina wafers, and blanched vegetables such as courgette and cucumber. Protein-heavy feeding should be minimised; a plant-dominated diet mirrors its natural foraging ecology.

Provide ample smooth rocks, slate pieces, and driftwood for grazing surfaces and sheltering spots. The species is generally peaceful and can be kept singly or in pairs in larger tanks, though conspecific males may compete for prime grazing territory. A tank of at least 120 L (90 cm footprint) with excellent filtration and weekly water changes of 25–30% is recommended. Chaetostoma milesi is copper-sensitive, as are many catfishes; copper-based medications must be avoided.

See also: [bristlenose-pleco] (Ancistrus cirrhosus) — the common beginner-friendly pleco with nasal tentacles; [hillstream-loach] (Sewellia lineolata) — similar stream-dwelling grazer with analogous requirements.
breedingNotes: Breeding Chaetostoma milesi in captivity is uncommon and generally considered difficult. Spawning has been reported in well-maintained aquaria that closely replicate natural conditions: cool, highly oxygenated, fast-flowing water with a seasonal drop in temperature toward 20°C, which appears to simulate the Andean dry-season conditions that may trigger spawning in the wild. Eggs are deposited in caves or under flat rocks, with the male guarding the clutch. Fry are relatively large at hatching and begin grazing on biofilm and algae immediately. Commercial breeding is rare and most trade specimens are wild-collected. Hobbyists attempting to breed this species should provide multiple cave-type spawning sites, a dedicated species tank or separate breeding setup, and a diet rich in algae and vegetables to condition the adults.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Chaetostoma milesi
primary.notes: Primary reference. Provided native range (Colombia/Venezuela, Orinoco/Magdalena drainages), temperature range (20–24°C), pH 6.5–7.5, hardness, adult size (to ~12–14 cm), diet (aufwuchs, algae, biofilm), hillstream-loach-like requirements, and breeding notes. Strongly emphasises oxygenation and current requirements.

additional.name: FishBase — Chaetostoma milesi
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), native range (Colombia, Venezuela), adult size, and diet classification (herbivore/aufwuchs). Cross-referenced scientific name validity and distribution data.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pleco Care Guide
additional.notes: General Loricariidae care context; confirmed diet recommendations (algae wafers, vegetables, biofilm), tank decor preferences (driftwood, rocks), and supplementary feeding guidance applicable to Chaetostoma species.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Catfish of the Month: Chaetostoma species
additional.notes: Species profile covering Chaetostoma genus husbandry; confirmed cool-water, high-oxygenation requirements, aufwuchs-grazing behaviour, bulldog head morphology, and distinction from Ancistrus (no nasal tentacles). Notes on conspecific territorial behaviour in smaller tanks.

additional.name: PlanetCatfish — Chaetostoma milesi (CW004)
additional.notes: Specialist catfish database entry; provided additional distribution data, hobbyist care reports, and confirmation of temperature sensitivity and oxygenation demands. Supplemented breeding difficulty assessment and note on wild-collection prevalence of trade specimens.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity. Spawning requires cool, highly oxygenated, fast-flowing water and a seasonal temperature drop toward 20°C to trigger spawning behaviour. The male guards the eggs inside a cave or under a flat rock. Most trade fish are wild-collected.
